indicators.name: AAII Bull-Bear Sentiment Spread
indicators.description: The weekly American Association of Individual Investors survey bull-minus-bear percentage. A contrarian indicator: extreme bearish readings have historically preceded positive forward returns; extreme bullish readings have preceded weaker returns.

criteria.description: Contrarian bullish trigger when the bull-bear spread falls below a deeply bearish threshold (for example, the 10th percentile of its historical distribution). Contrarian bearish trigger when it rises above the 90th percentile. Threshold values below are placeholders and need calibration.

current_state.notes: Stubbed. Needs a weekly scrape from AAII or an equivalent data source to evaluate.

historical_base_rates.notes: Not populated. AAII is a noisy series on any given week; it is most useful at extremes. Verify the conditional edge at your chosen threshold before relying on this record.

qualitative_note.text: Seeded as a placeholder so the library has a sentiment indicator alongside the quantitative volatility and trend signals. Retail sentiment surveys have well-known limitations: small sample size, self-selection, and weak signal outside of extreme readings. Treat the low confidence rating as load-bearing until the base rates are populated from your own study.

indicators.name: High-Yield Credit Spread (OAS)
indicators.description: The ICE BofA US High Yield Index option-adjusted spread over Treasuries. Widening spreads signal rising credit stress and correlate with equity drawdowns; narrowing spreads indicate risk appetite is returning.

criteria.description: A bullish equity regime is indicated when high-yield OAS is below a chosen risk threshold and trending lower. A bearish regime is indicated when OAS rises above the threshold and is trending higher. Thresholds below are placeholder and need calibration against your own study of historical regimes.

current_state.notes: Stubbed. Needs a FRED pull or equivalent data source to evaluate.

historical_base_rates.notes: Not populated. Expected to be strongly mean-reverting with useful predictive content at 3-6 month horizons, but any specific numbers should come from your own study before being trusted.

qualitative_note.text: Seeded as a placeholder so the library has at least one credit indicator alongside the volatility and trend signals. Replace the threshold, base rates and data wiring before relying on this record.

indicators.name: SPX Oversold-to-Overbought Transition Speed
indicators.description: A velocity-of-reversal indicator. When the S&P 500 transitions from a technically oversold state to an overbought state in an unusually small number of trading days, it signals aggressive buyer conviction after a capitulation low. Historically associated with durable lows and strong forward outperformance.

source.name: Self-forwarded note (original research provider not cited)
source.notes: The observation quoted reads: "Only the 1982 lows saw the S&P 500 move from oversold to overbought quicker (11 days this time versus the '82 record of six days). Quick moves like this are consistent with potential lows and strong forward outperformance." Primary source not attached. The operational definition of "oversold" and "overbought" used in the original study is not stated; a sensible default is the 14-day RSI crossing below 30 and then above 70, but this needs verification before the record is relied on.

criteria.description: Trigger when the number of trading days between the S&P 500's 14-day RSI closing below 30 (oversold) and subsequently closing above 70 (overbought) is less than or equal to a fast-transition threshold. The threshold below is a placeholder; calibrate against the distribution of historical transition counts.
criteria.machine:
oversold_day  = last day where SPX_RSI14 < 30
overbought_day = first day after oversold_day where SPX_RSI14 > 70
days_to_transition = overbought_day - oversold_day
trigger = days_to_transition <= 15

raw_values.historical_rank: 2nd fastest transition on record (1982 was 6 days)

current_state.notes: Status taken from self-forwarded note. Days-to-transition of 11 is recorded as the current reading; refresh requires either adopting the original study's definitions or reproducing them with RSI14.

historical_base_rates.notes: The note asserts a historical association with durable lows and strong forward outperformance but does not provide a conditional returns table. An event study of transitions under 15 days should produce 1m/3m/6m/12m median and mean returns with hit rates. Leave nulls until those are computed.

qualitative_note.text: This indicator captures the velocity of the rally off a low, which is conceptually distinct from a VIX-based volatility reset: a fast RSI-based round-trip can occur without the VIX ever crossing 20, and a VIX collapse can happen without the index making a clean oversold print. Treat the two as complementary rather than duplicative. Also note that the reference to "the 1982 lows" implies this is a rare event; small sample sizes deserve low confidence even when the directional claim is intuitively sensible.

indicators.name: SPX Trend Above 200-Day Moving Average
indicators.description: The S&P 500 closes at least 2% above its 200-day moving average, indicating that buyers have sufficient conviction to drive prices meaningfully above the long-term trend.

criteria.description: The S&P 500 closing price is greater than 1.02 times its 200-day simple moving average. Most useful when combined with a volatility-regime indicator such as VIX < 20.

current_state.notes: Status taken from SentimenTrader note. Populate raw values from data feed on refresh.

historical_base_rates.notes: When a streak of VIX prints above 20 ends and SPX is simultaneously trading at least 2% above its 200-day MA, forward returns have historically improved dramatically. The note is explicit that the setup also fired during the early stages of the 2001-02, 2008 and 2022 bear markets, so the signal is not without notable failures. Conditional median/mean forward returns at 1m/3m/6m/12m were not extracted and need verification before use.

qualitative_note.text: The source note emphasises that this trend condition works best paired with the VIX Calm Regime indicator. Either condition alone is weaker; both conditions off (VIX > 20 and SPX below 200DMA) is historically the worst regime to be long.

indicators.name: VIX Two-Week Crash Magnitude
indicators.description: A magnitude-based measure of volatility collapse. The rolling two-week percentage decline in the VIX is ranked against its full history; the largest declines are associated with capitulation in volatility after a stress episode and have historically preceded positive forward returns.

source.name: Self-forwarded note (original research provider not cited)
source.notes: The observation quoted reads: "The 38% decline in the VIX over the last 2 weeks is the 7th biggest volatility crash in history." No primary source was attached. Provenance to be filled in if the original chart or note is located.

criteria.description: Trigger when the rolling two-week percentage decline in the VIX places in the top decile of its historical distribution. The 10% threshold is a placeholder; the specific rank that maps to "top 10 in history" should be calibrated against your own series.

current_state.notes: Status and magnitude taken from the self-forwarded note. Populate from a live data feed on refresh and re-rank against the current history.

historical_base_rates.notes: The note claims this is the 7th biggest volatility crash in history but does not supply conditional forward-return statistics. A clean event study should compute median and mean forward returns at 1m/3m/6m/12m conditional on the top-decile event, with a hit rate. Do not rely on this record until those numbers are populated.

qualitative_note.text: This indicator is orthogonal to vix-calm-regime (a threshold on the level) and vix-range-rank-reversal (a statistical pattern in the four-month range). It measures the speed of the volatility collapse rather than its level or pattern. Firing alongside the other two VIX indicators gives a stronger combined signal, but also raises the possibility that the three are simply rhyming facets of the same underlying event rather than three independent confirmations.

indicators.name: VIX Calm Regime
indicators.description: VIX closes below the 20 threshold, marking a transition from a volatile "panic" regime into a "calm" regime in which institutions and retail participants are more likely to deploy capital.

criteria.description: The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) closes below 20 after having been above 20 for an extended period.

raw_values.VIX_close: < 20 (per note, exact value to be refreshed from data feed)

current_state.notes: Status taken from SentimenTrader note. Needs a live data feed to be authoritative; populate raw_values.VIX_close with the actual print on refresh.

historical_base_rates.notes: When VIX is below 20, the S&P 500 next-day total returns have historically been more than 1.5 times the unconditional average. Specific forward-return medians for 1m/3m/6m/12m were not extracted from the note and should be verified before use.

qualitative_note.text: SentimenTrader flags that the V-shaped rally preceding this signal has been unusually aggressive, which may have pulled forward some near-term upside. The signal itself is valid; immediate expectations should be tempered and a brief consolidation would not be unusual.

indicators.name: VIX Range Rank Reversal Buy Signal
indicators.description: A quantitative reversal signal that fires when the VIX 4-month range rank violently resets from a period of extreme volatility (rank > 99.9) to a calm regime (rank < 40), provided the SPY 5-day rate of change is positive within 10 trading days of the reset.

criteria.description: A buy signal is triggered when all four conditions below are satisfied in sequence. Conditions 3 and 4 introduce a 10-day window from the moment the range rank drops below 40.
criteria.machine:
C1: VIX_4mo_range_rank crosses above 99.9
C2: VIX_4mo_range_rank then crosses below 40
C3: days_since_C2 <= 10
C4: SPY_5d_roc > 0%
trigger = C1 and C2 and C3 and C4

current_state.notes: Signal state taken from the source note. Needs a proper implementation of the 4-month range rank calculation to refresh autonomously.

historical_base_rates.notes: The note characterises historical performance as "incredibly solid" but does not supply a clean table of conditional medians. The most recent prior signal triggered in November of the preceding year, posting +2.3% over the first month before softening. Population of specific 1m/3m/6m/12m numbers requires access to the SentimenTrader back-test or an independent replication of the range-rank rule.

qualitative_note.text: The source explicitly tempers expectations for this specific firing: the current V-shaped bounce has been even more aggressive than the previous instance, so the market may have pulled forward a significant portion of the near-term upside the signal would ordinarily promise. Historical win rates still support the direction; the size of the immediate payoff is what to discount.
